title: Investment Fundamentals
description: Dive into fundamental analysis, dividend yield, portfolio rebalancing, ETFs vs mutual funds, REITs, and market cycles.

blocks.text: Fundamental Analysis: The P/E Ratio Intermediate investors move beyond "gut feelings" to Fundamental Analysis. The Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio is a key metric used to determine if a stock is overvalued or undervalued, calculated by dividing the share price by the earnings per share (EPS). A high P/E might suggest high future growth expectations, while a low P/E could indicate a "value" bargain or a company in trouble. Comparing a company’s P/E to its industry average helps you decide if the price you are paying is reasonable. Action: Select a major company listed on Tadawul or Amman Stock Exchange and find its current P/E ratio. Compare it to a competitor in the same sector.

Understanding Dividend Yield For many investors in the MENA region, cash flow is as important as price growth. Dividend Yield is a financial ratio that shows how much a company pays out in dividends each year relative to its stock price, expressed as a percentage. This represents a form of passive income. Intermediate investors use this metric to compare the "cash return" of a stock to the interest they would receive from a standard bank account. Action: Identify a high-dividend-paying company in your local market and calculate its yield based on the last four quarterly payouts.

Correlation and Portfolio Rebalancing Diversification is only effective if your assets are not highly correlated—meaning they don't all move in the same direction at the same time. Intermediate investors look for low correlation, such as simultaneously owning gold, real estate, and stocks. Furthermore, you must rebalance your portfolio annually to return to your original risk level. If stocks grow to represent 80% of your wealth instead of a target 60%, you should sell some and buy other assets to maintain balance. Action: Review your current investments. Are they all in the same industry or country? If so, identify one asset class that usually moves differently.

Asset Allocation is the process of deciding how to distribute your portfolio across different asset classes like stocks, bonds, and cash. It is the most significant factor in determining your long-term returns and risk levels, far outweighing individual stock selection.

Intermediate investors choose an allocation based on their "Time Horizon." Generally, the further you are from retirement, the more you can afford to hold in high-volatility, high-growth assets like stocks, while those closer to retirement shift toward the stability of bonds and cash.
Action: Define your target allocation (e.g., 70% Stocks, 20% Real Estate, 10% Gold/Cash).

lessons.title: The Difference Between ETFs and Mutual Funds

sections.title: The Difference Between ETFs and Mutual Funds

blocks.text: Mutual funds are managed by people and often carry high fees, while Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) usually track an index automatically at a much lower cost. These low costs are vital because even a 1% difference in fees can wipe out up to 25% of your potential wealth over 30 years.

Intermediate investors prioritize ETFs for their efficiency, transparency, and liquidity. By choosing low-fee index tracking, you are essentially "buying the whole market" rather than betting on a single manager's ability to beat the odds.
Action: Look up the "Expense Ratio" of your current investment funds. Aim for anything below 0.5%.

REITs are companies that own, operate, or finance income-producing real estate. They are traded on stock exchanges, providing intermediate investors with the ability to gain real estate exposure with much higher liquidity than buying a physical building.

By owning REITs, you can earn rental-like dividends without the headaches of being a landlord. It is an excellent way to diversify a stock portfolio with an asset class that often moves independently of the general market.
Action: Find one REIT listed on your local exchange and check its "Dividend Yield."
Markets move in cycles: Bull (rising) and Bear (falling). Investor sentiment—the collective fear and greed of the market—often drives prices far above or below their actual value during these cycles.

Intermediate investors learn to ignore the daily "noise" and remain disciplined. They use Dollar-Cost Averaging (investing the same amount every month) to ensure they are buying more shares when prices are low and fewer when they are high, effectively removing emotion from the process.
Action: Commit to a fixed monthly investment amount regardless of whether the news says the market is "up" or "down."
Market Cap is the total value of all a company's shares. Companies are generally grouped into Large-Cap (stable giants), Mid-Cap, and Small-Cap (high-growth, high-risk).

Intermediate investors ensure they aren't over-concentrated in just one size. While Large-Caps provide stability and dividends, Small-Caps provide the explosive growth potential that can significantly boost a portfolio’s overall performance over decades.
Action: Check the Market Cap of the top three companies in your portfolio and see if you are over-concentrated in one size.

To protect wealth from the erosion of purchasing power, intermediate investors hold "Inflation Hedges." Gold has maintained its value over centuries, while TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities) are bonds that adjust their principal based on inflation rates.

Having 5% to 10% of your portfolio in these assets acts as an "insurance policy" for your wealth. During periods of high inflation where stocks might struggle, these assets often perform well, providing a vital stabilizing force for your net worth.
Action: Research the current price of gold in your local currency and its performance over the last 5 years compared to your savings account return.
An IPS is a personal "rulebook" for your investments. It defines your goals, your risk tolerance, and the specific rules for when you will buy, sell, or rebalance your portfolio.

Having a written IPS is the final step in moving from a casual saver to a professional investor. It prevents you from making emotional decisions during market crashes, ensuring you stay committed to your long-term plan regardless of short-term volatility.
Action: Write down three rules for your portfolio (e.g., "I will never sell during a market drop of less than 20%").
